zotero.abstractNote: Feature attribution methods are widely used to explain the predictions of machine learning models. However, these methods often struggle to distinguish between the direct influence of a feature and its indirect influence mediated by dependencies with other features. In this paper, we propose a framework to disentangle these effects. We define a causal notion of feature attribution that separates the direct effect of a feature from the effects mediated by other features. We show that existing attribution methods can be interpreted as different ways of aggregating these effects, and we provide a unified perspective that clarifies their differences. We also propose a new attribution method that explicitly disentangles these effects and demonstrate its effectiveness on synthetic and real-world datasets.
